editing_steps: I'm so excited to guide you through the basics of CapCut and help you get up and running in no time.
If you're new to video editing, CapCut is the perfect choice.
It's simple, powerful, and doesn't require the endless hours of running complicated software.
In one of the best parts, you can choose to use CapCut on your desktop or use the mobile version on your phone or tablet.
The CapCut mobile is amazing for on-the-go edits and quick changes.
While CapCut Desktop gives you a lot more screen space for detailed editing.
Try both, see which one fits your style and workflow and just stick with what feels best.
Just remember, don't get caught up on overthinking and just keep moving forward because things will become clearer as you take action.
CapCut also offers a free version that's packed with features.
If you're just starting out, the free version has everything you need.
But of course, the big question I always get is do I need the pro version?
The answer is no.
If you don't want it, then you don't need it.
The pro version comes with extra perks, but trust me, most of what you need to create eye-catching videos is already there for free.
The courses are not reliant on the pro version.
If you ever see me demonstrating a pro effect like the background removal, I'll always have a free alternative solution for you.
Whether you're building a brand, boosting your business, or you just want to get creative, CapCut makes it easy to make stunning videos without all the hassle.
Grab your device and let's get started.

Hello, creative minds.
By the end of this module you'll have a solid understanding of what masking is and how to use it to add a creative touch to your videos.
Let's dive in.
First off let's demystify what masking is.
In the realm of video editing, masking allows you to isolate and manipulate specific areas of your video.
Masking is like the artist brush and video editing.
It enables you to blend reveal, hide and create intriguing visuals.
Sometimes masking is used practically to fix things, sometimes it's used as more of a creative tool.
Imagine you wanna reveal text in your video but not the usual pop up way you can have text appear behind an object using masking to integrate that text in the scene and make it much more engaging.
By isolating specific areas you can blend different video clips seamlessly, adding a cinematic touch to your content.

Let's say you have a clip and you wanna adjust the color on only one element in the frame, masking allows you to isolate that element and apply color correction making it stand out or blend differently with the environment.
May seem a bit complex at first but with practice you'll be creating stunning and visually rich content in no time.
I'll show you some examples in the next video.
Let's take a look at a couple of simple layers here.
We have this black layer and a white layer.
To demonstrate masking I'm gonna take my white layer overlay it to drop it down and we'll put it right on top of the black layer.
Now all we're seeing is white, okay.
If we go to the mask tool and we select Split Mask, it's gonna split that layer right in half, so now we're seeing the white layer on the top.
The top half of the screen and the black layer on the bottom half.

If we move that back and forth kind of see what's happening there it's almost like a doorway or a window so wherever we don't have the mask we're seeing through to the black layer which is actually behind the white layer.
So with our masks selected we can move that around, we can use our fingers to rotate the mask so if you want it to be diagonal or we can change it so now if we change it to a circle mask.
It's creating a little bit of a window there so wherever we move that circle is where we're gonna see the top layer.
If we wanted to swap it and actually use that as a window to see through to the bottom layer we would click invert down here in the bottom left corner and it would reverse the position of the mask.
If we wanted it to smoothly transition from one side to the other we would use the feather here and now we have a smoother outline on that mask.
We can change it to a rectangle and if we click invert again now we're seeing that white layer on top.
Just like if we had a cake and we were looking down on it, the top layer is what we would see first, right?

If we cut it out that's like creating a mask where this comes in handy and the mask that I use the most is the split mask and it would be if I have two clips that are trying to blend together and I want one to show on the left and one to show on the right so basically we would take this split mask and we would rotate it.
Usually would have some type of feather to smooth that so that you wouldn't see the harsh edge in the middle of the two clips and it would blend a lot smoother.
So that's the basics of what a mask is used for.

editing_steps: By the end of this module you'll understand why they are necessary and how to use keyframes to make your content more engaging and dynamic.
Let's jump in.
What are keyframes?
Think of them as markers that help us create animations by defining start and endpoints of any movement or change.
They're like the secret sauce to making your videos come alive.
Using keyframes can totally elevate your content.
They allow for smooth transitions, movements and changes ultimately making your videos more engaging.
Imagine you're creating a video you wanna draw attention to a specific detail on the screen here's where key frames come in handy.
You can set keyframes to smoothly zoom in and out, focusing on the detail and then returning to the full view.
Adding text overlays to your videos you can set keyframes to adjust the opacity making your text appear and disappear smoothly.
Important to note that if you want quicker success, practice, practice, practice and repeat.
Experiment with them, play around and see how they can elevate your content, then come back watch these videos again as you run into issues what you will do at first.

Flashlight Costume Change - Edit - Capcut Desktop
Costume Fly-in Effect - Capcut Desktop (pro feature used)
FREE Alternative to the Remove Background tool

filming_steps: Let's talk about shooting green screen and camera settings.
Exposure Adjust the camera settings to avoid over exposing or under exposing the green screen the goal is to capture a vibrant uniform green that can easily be heat up.
Focus Be mindful depth of field.
Subject should be in sharp focus while ensuring the green screen remains a consistent color throughout the scene.
Resolution In general you wanna have sharper footage to work with when pulling a key.
Frame Rate and Shutter Speed Motion blur can be problematic and cause less than appealing results.
Higher frame rates and shutter speed will help eliminate motion blur.
If your subject is moving around a lot you're gonna wanna opt for higher frame rates and shutter speeds 30 frames per second or higher.
If shooting with your phone you may want to use a third party app so you have full control over your settings.

Background and Foreground Integration Lens Selection Depending on the size of your green screen you'll need to consider the focal length of your lens if you have a small screen you typically need to place your camera farther back from the subject with a larger focal length to keep the subject visually within the screen.
If using your cell phone you might be able to just pull your tripod back and change it to 2 times zoom if you have a larger screen you'll have more flexibility and creative control.
Matching Elements When planning your shots consider how the subjects will interact with the intended background and post production.
This includes matching the direction of the lighting, the color temperature and perspective of the camera.
If you can use the same lighting setup for both scenes for a seamless integration.
editing_steps: A green screen is a very common piece of the VFX puzzle but it can also be the most intimidating, whether you're a filmmaker, content creator or just curious about how visual effects are made you're in the right place.
Green screen technology is a powerful tool in the creative industry.
Allowing you to transform your storytelling and bring your wildest imaginations to life and they will be diving into a lot of details because understanding the nuances really does make a difference.
We want you to know that it's totally okay if you're just starting out.

You might be thinking this sounds complicated but don't worry our goal is to make it as user friendly as possible and make sure to include simple examples to get you up and running quickly from choosing your green screen to understanding lighting and positioning your subject we got you covered step by step.
One of the most important takeaways from this module will be the role of lighting.
Proper lighting can significantly reduce your editing time and if done right your audience won't even know the difference.
Lighting is also one of the main factors that can make or break your effect.
Applying these basic principles will keep us in the right direction.
That being said don't be afraid to jump in and start experimenting.
We learn from practice not from watching.
Your experience with green screens does not matter, starting with even the simplest setup can lead to incredible results and just remember every great creator started somewhere.
Throughout this module we encourage you to test out projects explore your creativity and see for yourself how green screen technology can elevate your work.
We're here to help you every step of the way.
Are you ready to unlock the full potential of your content?
Let's dive in and make some magic happen together.
Remember to take actions first then ask questions later.
What is a green screen?
A green screen is a backdrop used in visual effects to make it simpler to isolate the foreground and replace the background with any digital image.
The technique known as chromaking.
Lets you create scenes that would otherwise be impossible or too expensive to shoot in real life.
The science behind color separation.
The selection of green for green screens is grounded in the science of color separation.
Digital cameras capture images using red green and blue channels.
Green is used most frequently because digital sensors are most sensitive to green wavelengths and it provides a high level of luminance compared to other colors.
This makes the green color stand out more distinctly allowing for easier and cleaner separation from the subject.
The luminance factor is crucial for defining the edges around the subjects during the keen process reducing the amount of spill and making the compositing process more straightforward.
Alternating colors.
While green screens are widely used blue screens are also a viable option and were in fact the original choice for film compositing before digital technology.
The choice between green and blue screens depends on several factors.
Costume and subject color.
If the subject or objects in the scene are green or contain green such as plants a blue screen may be used to avoid king out these elements.
Lighting conditions.
Blue screens can be more beneficial and low light conditions as they require less light to illuminate effectively.
Digital sensors and film.
Depending on the type of camera and the specific requirement of the shoot blue screens may offer advantages in terms of color reproduction and sensor sensitivity.
Choosing the right green screen.
Green screens come in various materials including cloth, paper and paint.
Cloth screens are portable and can be easily hung or stretched to remove wrinkles.
Paper rolls are good disposable option for a clean seamless look but can tear easily.
Paint can transform any wall or flat surface into a permanent green screen ideal for dedicated studio spaces.
Size and Space Requirements Size of your green screen should be based on the framing of your shots and the movement of your subject.
Make sure it's wide and tall enough to cover the entire background and your cameras feel the view with extra space for subjects to move around.
Key considerations before purchasing.
Opt for a wrinkle resistant material to avoid shadows and uneven lighting.
Bright chroma key is typically the best choice for phones and digital cameras.
Assess your space and needs, check portable versus permanent setup.
Lighting Your Green Screen Proper lighting is crucial in green screen work to ensure a clean key minimizing post production work and achieve a seamless blend between the subject and the background.
I'm gonna talk about some techniques that apply across the board and then we'll compare indoor to outdoor setups.
Even Illumination The green screen must be lit evenly to avoid shadows and hotspots.
This facilitates a smoother king process and post production.
Use soft diffuse light sources placed symmetrically on both sides of the screen to achieve uniform lighting.
Separate Subject Lighting Let your subject independently from the green screen to control shadows and avoid green spill.
The separation ensures the subject is properly exposed and the green screen's color does not reflect onto the subject.
Avoiding Shadows and Spill Position the subject at least 6 feet from the green screen to minimize shadows and green spill.
Use back lighting or rim light to create a subtle separation between the subject and the background.
Using Flags and Reflectors Utilize flags to block unwanted light from hitting the screen and reflectors to fill the shadows of the subject without affecting the green screens lighting.
Types of Light and Placement Soft diffused lighting works best to avoid hotspots and shadows on the green screen.
LED panels or soft boxes placed on either side of the screen can provide even illumination.
The goal is to light the screen separately from the subject to maintain consistent lighting across the entire background.
Avoiding Shadows and Hot Spots Position lights at a 45 degree angle to the screen ensuring their evenly spaced and aimed to cover the screen uniformly.
Use additional lighting to eliminate shadows cast by the subject.
Be mindful of the distance and intensity of the lights to avoid hotspots which making difficult.
Ensuring Even Elimination Adjust the lights is needed to eliminate any dark or overly bright areas the more evenly lit the screen the easier it will be to key it out in post production.
Indoor Lighting Indoor setups offer controlled conditions making it easier to achieve consistent lighting.
Light Sources Soft boxes and LED panels are ideal for indoor use they provide soft even light they can be easily controlled and directed.
Screen Lighting Place two light sources at a 45 degree angle to the green screen.
Ensuring their equal power and distance from the screen to avoid gradients and shadows.
Subject Lighting Use a 3 point lighting setup to illuminate the subject this setup highlights the subjects form and separates them from the background.
Outdoor Lighting Outdoor green screen shoots can be challenging due to the variability of natural light and environmental conditions.
Timing Overcast days are ideal for outdoor green screen work because the cloud cover offers diffuse natural lighting that evenly lights the screen.
Using Natural Light as Fill Use the sun as a backlight or a side light and use reflectors or diffusers to fill in the shadows.
This approach can help maintain even lighting on the screen.
Wind Instability Secure your green screen and lighting equipment properly to handle wind and other outdoor elements use sandbags and clamps to stabilize your setup.
Environmental Considerations Be mindful of changing light conditions and environmental reflections that affect your lighting setup.
Regularly monitor and adjust your setup as needed throughout the shoot.
Let's talk about positioning your subject.
The Distance from the Screen Keeping your subject at least 6 feet from the green screen minimizes green spill which can make king a background more challenging.
This distance also helps reduce shadows cast on the screen.
Minimizing Green Spill Aside from maintaining distance lighting the subject separately from your green screen can further minimize spill.
Use flags or gobos to block the green light from hitting your subject directly.
Properly positioning and diffusing your subjects lights can also prevent spill.
Conclusion Whether you're working indoors with controlled lighting or outdoors with the challenges of natural light the key to successful green screen lighting lies in achieving even elimination.
Separating subject lighting from the screen and minimizing shadows and spill.
By mastering these techniques you'll ensure a high quality key and a more professional final product regardless of your filming environment.

lessons.title: Shooting With Green Screen
lessons.transcript:
Let's talk about shooting green screen and camera settings. Exposure Adjust the camera settings to avoid over exposing or under exposing the green screen the goal is to capture a vibrant uniform green that can easily be heat up. Focus Be mindful depth of field. Subject should be in sharp focus while ensuring the green screen remains a consistent color throughout the scene.

Resolution In general you wanna have sharper footage to work with when pulling a key. Frame Rate and Shutter Speed Motion blur can be problematic and cause less than appealing results. Higher frame rates and shutter speed will help eliminate motion blur. If your subject is moving around a lot you're gonna wanna opt for higher frame rates and shutter speeds 30 frames per second or higher.

If shooting with your phone you may want to use a third party app so you have full control over your settings. Background and Foreground Integration Lens Selection Depending on the size of your green screen you'll need to consider the focal length of your lens if you have a small screen you typically need to place your camera farther back from the subject with a larger focal length to keep the subject visually within the screen. So if using your cell phone you might be able to just pull your tripod back and change it to 2 times zoom if you have a larger screen you'll have more flexibility and creative control.

Matching Elements When planning your shots consider how the subjects will interact with the intended background and post production. This includes matching the direction of the lighting, the color temperature and perspective of the camera. If you can use the same lighting setup for both scenes for a seamless integration.

Scroll to the point where you want the speed effect to begin.
Tap split then scroll to the point where you want the speed effect to end tap split again.
With the middle section selected tap speed then boost it to 15 times or more.

Should look like this with the same section selected tap motion blur boost the blur all the way up change the blending to about 20 change the time setting to twice this will double the blur amount and that's it.

effects.gear: Tripod, a wall/flat surface, hologram or HUD stock footage (e.g. free ProductionCrate elements).
effects.technique_note: The vault ships only a demo clip here — this is the standard Screen-blend overlay method for holograms.
filming_steps: Lock the camera on a tripod facing the wall where the hologram will appear.
Act out reacting to / interacting with the empty wall so your eyeline and gestures sell it.
editing_steps: Import your clip, then add the hologram stock footage as an Overlay layer.
Set the overlay's Blend mode to SCREEN — this drops the black background so only the glowing hologram shows.
Scale and position it onto the wall; use keyframes if the camera or subject moves.
Mask the overlay if part of the hologram should be hidden behind you.
Add a subtle blue glow / sci-fi sound effect to finish.

effects.gear: Phone on a tripod (locked off — never move it), good even lighting.
effects.technique_note: The vault ships only a demo clip for this one — this is the standard locked-off cloning method that produces the exact result shown.
filming_steps: Mount your phone on a tripod and frame your scene. Do NOT move the camera between takes.
Take 1: stand/act in position A, then walk out of frame.
Take 2: without moving the camera, sit/act in position B, walk out.
Take 3: act in position C. Keep positions far enough apart that the clones don't overlap.
Use the same exposure/focus lock for every take so lighting matches.
editing_steps: Import all 3 clips into CapCut and stack them as overlays (clip 1 on the main track, clips 2 and 3 as Overlay layers above).
Because each clone is in a different part of the frame, you usually only need to MASK each overlay to reveal its clone.
Add a Rectangle or Brush mask to overlay 2 over the area where that clone sits; feather the edge slightly.
Repeat for overlay 3. Now all three 'you's appear at once.
Trim so the action lines up, then add audio.

effects.gear: Tripod, water/pond or reflection stock footage.
effects.technique_note: The vault ships only a demo clip here — this is the standard masked water-overlay method.
filming_steps: Lock the camera on a tripod looking at the patch of ground/yard you want to turn into water.
editing_steps: Overlay the water/pond stock footage above your shot.
Mask the water overlay to the exact area that should become the pond; feather the edge.
Lower opacity slightly and match color so it blends with the ground.
Add a reflection (duplicate scenery, flip vertically, mask into the water) and ripple/ambient sound.

effects.gear: Tripod, storm-cloud / time-lapse sky stock footage.
effects.technique_note: The vault ships only a demo clip here — this is the standard sky-replacement method.
filming_steps: Lock the camera on a tripod with the sky visible in frame.
A static shot makes the sky replacement far easier.
editing_steps: Add the storm-cloud footage as an Overlay above your clip.
Mask out the existing sky (mask the overlay to the sky region, or use Cutout if there's a clean horizon).
Set blend/opacity so the new clouds sit naturally; keyframe slow drift.
Cool the color grade and add wind/thunder SFX.

effects.gear: Two locations (e.g. ground + rooftop/ledge), tripod or steady hands.
effects.technique_note: The vault ships only a demo clip here — this is the standard match-cut jump method.
filming_steps: Location A: film yourself crouching and JUMPING UP, exiting the top of frame.
Location B: film yourself DROPPING INTO frame and landing, matching the same body angle.
Match framing and motion direction at the cut point (the apex of the jump).
editing_steps: Place clip A then clip B on the timeline.
Trim clip A to the moment you leave frame at the top.
Trim the start of clip B to the moment you enter from the top.
Cut them together at that apex — the match cut makes one continuous super-jump.
Add a whoosh/impact SFX on the landing and a tiny speed ramp for punch.

effects.technique_note: The vault ships only an inspiration reel here — this is the standard beat-synced reveal method.
filming_steps: Film a short clip of each product (or each angle) with identical framing and lighting.
Add a consistent motion you can transition on — e.g. swipe a hand across, or a quick push toward the lens.
editing_steps: Lay the 3 product clips in sequence.
Add a quick transition between each (zoom/spin/swipe) timed to the motion or a beat.
Sync the cuts to a trending audio track's beats.
Add text/price callouts on each reveal.

effects.gear: Phone, two scenes you want to connect.
effects.technique_note: The vault ships only a demo clip here — this is the standard whip-pan sky transition.
filming_steps: Scene 1: whip-pan UP to the sky (fast tilt) at the end of the shot.
Scene 2: start on the sky and whip-pan DOWN into the new location.
Keep the pan speed/direction matching between the two.
editing_steps: Place scene 1 then scene 2 on the timeline.
Cut at the fastest, most-blurred frame of each pan (when it points at plain sky).
Add a quick blur or directional-blur transition over the cut.
Optionally mask the sky so the new scene's sky bleeds in early.

effects.technique_note: The vault marks the full tutorial 'coming soon' — this is the standard locked-off switch method.
filming_steps: Lock the camera. Hold object A still in frame.
Pass a hand / quick motion across the object, OR briefly cover it.
Without moving the camera, swap to object B in the exact same position and repeat the motion.
editing_steps: Place both clips on the timeline.
Cut at the frame where the hand fully covers the object (the wipe moment).
Align object B's clip so the hand reveal lines up — the object appears to transform.
Add a magic/whoosh SFX on the switch.

effects.gear: Tripod (locked off), the object, a clean-plate shot.
effects.technique_note: The vault marks the full tutorial 'coming soon' — this is the standard keyframed clean-plate method.
filming_steps: Lock the camera and film the scene WITH the object sitting still.
Film a clean plate of the same scene WITHOUT the object (for patching).
Optionally film yourself reacting to the 'moving' object.
editing_steps: Put the clean plate on the main track and the object clip as an Overlay.
Mask/cut out just the object from the overlay.
Use KEYFRAMES on the object overlay's Position to animate it sliding/creeping across the scene.
Add ease and a subtle shadow that moves with it; finish with an eerie SFX.
